summary: Do not ship. The locked RED record matches `red.sha`, and both committed call-site assertions fail for the intended `PASS ABORTED` symptom. However, option C still permits change-induced data loss. Simpler alternative: none found that preserves the declared all-call-site flip and data-loss constraint; a Temp-only catch leaves the same invariant broken. Open question: none—the human already ruled data loss unacceptable.

findings.title: A genuine vanished entry can still unlock same-pass deletion of a restored blob
findings.body: The plan claims same-pass reaping of restored blob S requires a forged `Gone`. It does not: the intended true-`Gone` flip is sufficient. References and pending tombstones are captured before the entry loop. If the first snapshotted blob X genuinely vanishes, baseline aborts at X, but option C continues. A later restored blob S with an expired tombstone and a pointer restoration occurring after reference collection is then grave/reaped; `pending.remove(X)` cannot protect S. This produces the permanent 404 that the human decision declared unacceptable and invalidates the plan's “data loss paths zero” conclusion.
findings.recommendation: Add a deterministic production-call-site witness with at least two expired blobs: park on the first `pre_entry`, make that entry genuinely vanish, restore a pointer for a later blob after reference collection, and assert the later blob is not reaped. Redesign the pass so any observed `Gone`, including during grave recovery, prevents stale pre-pass tombstones from authorizing subsequent irreversible reaps—likely by deferring reaps until the scan completes or implementing generation-bound tombstones.

next_steps: Revise the P-20 analysis to include true `Gone` paths, not only forged `Gone`.
Do not advance to implementation until the new data-loss witness and preservation rule are specified.
